title: Viola

infobox.value: ([/viˈoʊlə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*vee-OH-lə*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"),[[1]](#cite_note-1) ([ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-viola.ogg "File:En-us-viola.ogg")) Italian: [[ˈvjɔːla, viˈɔːla]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian")) is a [string instrument](/wiki/String_instrument "String instrument") of the [violin family](/wiki/Violin_family "Violin family"), and is usually [bowed](/wiki/Bow_(music) "Bow (music)") when played. The viola is slightly larger than the [violin](/wiki/Violin "Violin") and has a lower sound. Since the 18th century, it has been the middle or [alto](/wiki/Alto "Alto") voice of the [violin family](/wiki/Violin_family "Violin family"), between the violin (which is tuned a [perfect fifth](/wiki/Perfect_fifth "Perfect fifth") higher) and the [cello](/wiki/Cello "Cello") (which is tuned an [octave](/wiki/Octave "Octave") lower).[[2]](#cite_note-2) The strings from low to high are typically tuned to [C3, G3, D4, and A4](/wiki/Scientific_pitch_notation "Scientific pitch notation").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Bowed string instrument

This article is about the musical instrument. For other uses, see [Viola (disambiguation)](/wiki/Viola_(disambiguation) "Viola (disambiguation)").

For similar instruments, see [Violin](/wiki/Violin "Violin") and [Viol](/wiki/Viol "Viol").

The **viola** ([/viˈoʊlə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*vee-OH-lə*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"),[[1]](#cite_note-1) ([ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-viola.ogg "File:En-us-viola.ogg")) Italian: [[ˈvjɔːla, viˈɔːla]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian")) is a [string instrument](/wiki/String_instrument "String instrument") of the [violin family](/wiki/Violin_family "Violin family"), and is usually [bowed](/wiki/Bow_(music) "Bow (music)") when played. The viola is slightly larger than the [violin](/wiki/Violin "Violin") and has a lower sound. Since the 18th century, it has been the middle or [alto](/wiki/Alto "Alto") voice of the [violin family](/wiki/Violin_family "Violin family"), between the violin (which is tuned a [perfect fifth](/wiki/Perfect_fifth "Perfect fifth") higher) and the [cello](/wiki/Cello "Cello") (which is tuned an [octave](/wiki/Octave "Octave") lower).[[2]](#cite_note-2) The strings from low to high are typically tuned to [C3, G3, D4, and A4](/wiki/Scientific_pitch_notation "Scientific pitch notation").

In the past, the viola varied in size and style, as did its names. The word *viola* originates from the Italian language. The Italians often used the term *[viola da braccio](/wiki/Viola_da_braccio "Viola da braccio")*, meaning, literally, 'of the arm'. "Brazzo" was another Italian word for the viola, which the Germans adopted as *Bratsche*. The French had their own names: *cinquiesme* was a small viola, *haute contre* was a large viola, and *taile* was a [tenor](/wiki/Tenor "Tenor"). Today, the French use the term *alto*, a reference to its range.

The viola was popular in the heyday of five-part [harmony](/wiki/Harmony "Harmony"), up until the eighteenth century, taking three lines of the harmony and occasionally playing the [melody](/wiki/Melody "Melody") line. Music notation for the viola differs from most other instruments in that it primarily uses the [alto clef](/wiki/Alto_clef "Alto clef"). When viola music has substantial sections in a higher register, it switches to the [treble clef](/wiki/Treble_clef "Treble clef") to make it easier to read.

The viola often plays the "inner voices" in [string quartets](/wiki/String_quartet "String quartet") and symphonic writing, and it is more likely than the first violin to play [accompaniment](/wiki/Accompaniment "Accompaniment") parts. The viola occasionally plays a major, soloistic role in orchestral or chamber music. Examples include the [symphonic poem](/wiki/Symphonic_poem "Symphonic poem") *[Don Quixote](/wiki/Don_Quixote_(Strauss) "Don Quixote (Strauss)")*, by [Richard Strauss](/wiki/Richard_Strauss "Richard Strauss"), the [13th Quartet](/wiki/String_Quartet_No._13_(Shostakovich) "String Quartet No. 13 (Shostakovich)") by [Dmitri Shostakovich](/wiki/Dmitri_Shostakovich "Dmitri Shostakovich"), and a symphony with a main viola line: *[Harold en Italie](/wiki/Harold_en_Italie "Harold en Italie")*, by [Hector Berlioz](/wiki/Hector_Berlioz "Hector Berlioz"). In the earlier part of the 20th century, more composers began to write for the viola, encouraged by the emergence of specialized soloists such as [Lionel Tertis](/wiki/Lionel_Tertis "Lionel Tertis") and [William Primrose](/wiki/William_Primrose "William Primrose"). English composers [Arthur Bliss](/wiki/Arthur_Bliss "Arthur Bliss"), [Edwin York Bowen](/wiki/Edwin_York_Bowen "Edwin York Bowen"), [Benjamin Dale](/wiki/Benjamin_Dale "Benjamin Dale"), [Frank Bridge](/wiki/Frank_Bridge "Frank Bridge"), [Benjamin Britten](/wiki/Benjamin_Britten "Benjamin Britten"), [Rebecca Clarke](/wiki/Rebecca_Clarke_(composer) "Rebecca Clarke (composer)") and [Ralph Vaughan Williams](/wiki/Ralph_Vaughan_Williams "Ralph Vaughan Williams") all wrote substantial [chamber](/wiki/Chamber_music "Chamber music") and concert works. Many of these pieces were commissioned by, or written for, Tertis. [William Walton](/wiki/William_Walton "William Walton"), [Bohuslav Martinů](/wiki/Bohuslav_Martin%C5%AF "Bohuslav Martinů"), [Tōru Takemitsu](/wiki/T%C5%8Dru_Takemitsu "Tōru Takemitsu"), [Tibor Serly](/wiki/Tibor_Serly "Tibor Serly"), [Alfred Schnittke](/wiki/Alfred_Schnittke "Alfred Schnittke"), and [Béla Bartók](/wiki/B%C3%A9la_Bart%C3%B3k "Béla Bartók") have written well-known viola concertos. The concerti by [Bartók](/wiki/B%C3%A9la_Bart%C3%B3k "Béla Bartók"), [Paul Hindemith](/wiki/Paul_Hindemith "Paul Hindemith"), [Carl Stamitz](/wiki/Carl_Stamitz "Carl Stamitz"), [Georg Philipp Telemann](/wiki/Georg_Philipp_Telemann "Georg Philipp Telemann"), and Walton are considered major works of the [viola repertoire](/wiki/Viola_repertoire "Viola repertoire"). [Hindemith](/wiki/Paul_Hindemith "Paul Hindemith"), who was a violist, wrote a substantial amount of music for viola, including the concerto *[Der Schwanendreher](/wiki/Der_Schwanendreher "Der Schwanendreher")*.

## Form

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Viola&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Form")]

See also: [Violin construction and mechanics](/wiki/Violin_construction_and_mechanics "Violin construction and mechanics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Viola_Bridge.jpg/250px-Viola_Bridge.jpg)](/wiki/File:Viola_Bridge.jpg)

Viola close up of bridge

The viola is similar in material and construction to the [violin](/wiki/Violin "Violin"). A full-size viola's body is between 25 and 100 mm (1 and 4 in) longer than the body of a full-size violin (i.e., between 38 and 46 cm [15–18 in]), with an average length of 41 cm (16 in). Violas for children typically start at 30 cm (12 in).[[3]](#cite_note-Oak1-3)[[note 1]](#cite_note-5) For centuries,[*[vague](/wiki/Wikipedia:Vagueness "Wikipedia:Vagueness")*] viola makers have experimented with the size and shape of violas, often to make a lighter instrument with shorter [string](/wiki/String_(music) "String (music)") lengths, but with a large enough [sound box](/wiki/Sound_box "Sound box") to retain the sound associated with the instrument. Prior to the 18th century, a large viola (known as a *tenor*) was designed to play lower notes. A smaller-sized viola (*alto viola*) was used for higher [register](/wiki/Register_(music) "Register (music)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Oak_Leaf_viola.jpg/250px-Oak_Leaf_viola.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oak_Leaf_viola.jpg)

Oak Leaf viola, Eric Benning, [Benning Violins](/wiki/Benning_Violins "Benning Violins")

Several experiments have intended to increase the size of the viola to improve its sound and harmony. [Hermann Ritter](/wiki/Hermann_Ritter "Hermann Ritter")'s *viola alta*, which measured about 48 cm (19 in), was intended for use in [Wagner](/wiki/Richard_Wagner "Richard Wagner")'s operas.[[5]](#cite_note-6) The [Tertis](/wiki/Lionel_Tertis "Lionel Tertis") model viola, which has wider bouts and deeper ribs to promote a better tone, is another slightly "nonstandard" shape that allows the player to use a larger instrument. Many experiments with the acoustics of a viola, particularly increasing the size of the body, have resulted in a much deeper tone, making it resemble the tone of a cello. Since many composers wrote for a traditional-sized viola, particularly in orchestral music, changes in the tone of a viola can have unintended consequences upon the balance in ensembles.

One of the most notable makers of violas of the twentieth century was Englishman [A. E. Smith](/wiki/A._E._Smith_(violin_maker) "A. E. Smith (violin maker)"), whose violas are sought after and highly valued. Many of his violas remain in Australia, his country of residence, where during some decades the violists of the [Sydney Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Sydney_Symphony_Orchestra "Sydney Symphony Orchestra") had a dozen of them in their section.

More recent (and more radically shaped) innovations have addressed the ergonomic problems associated with playing the viola by making it shorter and lighter, while finding ways to keep the traditional sound. These include the Otto Erdesz "cutaway" viola, which has one shoulder cut out to make shifting easier;[[6]](#cite_note-7) the "Oak Leaf" viola, which has two extra bouts; [viol](/wiki/Viol "Viol")-shaped violas such as [Joseph Curtin](/wiki/Joseph_Curtin "Joseph Curtin")'s "Evia" model, which also uses a moveable neck and maple-veneered [carbon fibre](/wiki/Carbon_fibre "Carbon fibre") back, to reduce weight:[[7]](#cite_note-8) violas played in the same manner as cellos (see [vertical viola](/wiki/Vertical_viola "Vertical viola")); and the eye-catching ["Dalí](/wiki/Salvador_Dal%C3%AD "Salvador Dalí")-esque" shapes of both Bernard Sabatier's violas in fractional sizes—which appear to have melted—and David Rivinus' *Pellegrina* model violas.[[8]](#cite_note-9)

Other experiments that deal with the "ergonomics vs. sound" problem have appeared. The American composer [Harry Partch](/wiki/Harry_Partch "Harry Partch") fitted a viola with a cello neck to allow the use of his 43-tone scale, called the "adapted viola". Luthiers have also created five-stringed violas,[[9]](#cite_note-10) which allow a greater playing range.

## Method of playing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Viola&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Method of playing")]

See also: [Violin technique](/wiki/Violin_technique "Violin technique")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Playing_viola.jpg/250px-Playing_viola.jpg)](/wiki/File:Playing_viola.jpg)

Playing a 43 cm (17 in) viola in 3rd position.

A person who plays the viola is called a *violist* or a *viola player*. The technique required for playing a viola has certain differences compared with that of a violin, partly because of its larger size: the notes are spread out further along the fingerboard, although they do not usually require different fingerings. The viola's less responsive strings and the heavier bow warrant a somewhat different bowing technique, and a violist has to lean more intensely on the strings.[[10]](#cite_note-11)

The viola is held in the same manner as the violin; however, due to its larger size, some adjustments must be made to accommodate. The viola, just like the violin, is placed on top of the left shoulder between the shoulder and the left side of the face (chin). Because of the viola's size, violists with short arms tend to use smaller-sized instruments for easier playing. The most immediately noticeable adjustments that a player accustomed to playing the violin has to make are to use wider-spaced fingerings. It is common for some players to use a wider and more intense [vibrato](/wiki/Vibrato "Vibrato") in the left hand, facilitated by employing the fleshier pad of the finger rather than the tip, and to hold the [bow](/wiki/Bow_(music) "Bow (music)") and right arm further away from the player's body. A violist must bring the left elbow further forward or around, so as to reach the lowest string, which allows the fingers to press firmly and create a clearer tone. Different positions are often used, including half position, and most commonly third position.

The viola is strung with thicker gauge strings than the violin.[[11]](#cite_note-12) This, combined with its larger size and lower pitch range, results in a deeper and mellower tone. However, the thicker strings also mean that the viola responds to changes in bowing more slowly. Practically speaking, if a violist and violinist are playing together, the violist must begin moving the bow a fraction of a second sooner than the violinist. The thicker strings also mean that more weight must be applied with the bow to make them vibrate.

The viola's [bow](/wiki/Bow_(music) "Bow (music)") has a wider band of horsehair than a violin's bow, which is particularly noticeable near the [frog](/wiki/Bow_(music)#The_modern_Western_bow "Bow (music)") (or *heel* in the UK). Viola bows, at 70–74 g (2.5–2.6 oz), are heavier than violin bows (58–61 g [2.0–2.2 oz]). The profile of the rectangular outside corner of a viola bow frog generally is more rounded than on violin bows.

## Tuning

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Viola&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Tuning")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Viola_peg_strings.jpg/120px-Viola_peg_strings.jpg)](/wiki/File:Viola_peg_strings.jpg)

"Normal" stringing shown here; some players reverse the G and C.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/First_positionB.png/120px-First_positionB.png)](/wiki/File:First_positionB.png)

First position viola fingerings

The viola's four strings are normally tuned in fifths: the lowest string is [C](/wiki/Scientific_pitch_notation "Scientific pitch notation") (an [octave](/wiki/Octave "Octave") below [middle C](/wiki/Middle_C "Middle C")), with G, D, and A above it. This tuning is exactly one fifth below the violin,[[12]](#cite_note-13) so that they have three strings in common—G, D, and A—and is one octave above the cello.

Each string of a viola is wrapped around a [peg](/wiki/Tuning_peg "Tuning peg") near the scroll and is tuned by turning the peg. Tightening the string raises the pitch; loosening the string lowers the pitch. The A string is normally tuned first, to the pitch of the ensemble: generally 400–442 Hz. The other strings are then tuned to it in intervals of [fifths](/wiki/Fifths_tuning "Fifths tuning"), usually by bowing two strings simultaneously. Most violas also have *adjusters*—*[fine tuners](/wiki/Tuning_mechanisms_for_stringed_instruments#Screw-and-lever_tuners "Tuning mechanisms for stringed instruments")* that make finer pitch adjustments. Some violists will choose to only have one fine tuner on the A string; others choose to have fine tuners on all strings. These adjust the tension of the string via rotating a small knob above the [tailpiece](/wiki/Tailpiece "Tailpiece"). Such tuning is generally easier to learn than using the pegs, and adjusters are usually recommended for younger players and put on smaller violas, though pegs and adjusters are usually used together. Some violists reverse the tuning of the C and G strings, so that the thicker C string does not turn so severe an angle over the [nut](/wiki/Nut_(instrumental) "Nut (instrumental)"), although this is rare.

Small, temporary tuning adjustments can also be made by stretching a string with the hand. A string may be tuned down by pulling it above the fingerboard, or tuned up by pressing the part of the string in the pegbox. These techniques may be useful in performances such as live concerts, reducing the ill effects of an out-of-tune string or viola until a proper opportunity to tune.

The tuning C–G–D–A is used for the great majority of all viola music.[[13]](#cite_note-14) However, other tunings are occasionally employed, both in [classical music](/wiki/European_classical_music "European classical music"), where the technique is known as *[scordatura](/wiki/Scordatura "Scordatura")*, and in some [folk](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") styles. [Mozart](/wiki/Wolfgang_Amadeus_Mozart "Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart"), in his [Sinfonia Concertante for Violin, Viola and Orchestra](/wiki/Sinfonia_Concertante_for_Violin,_Viola_and_Orchestra "Sinfonia Concertante for Violin, Viola and Orchestra") in E♭, wrote the viola part in D major, and specified that the violist raises the strings in pitch by a [semitone](/wiki/Semitone "Semitone"). He probably intended to give the viola a brighter tone so the rest of the orchestra would not overpower it. Tertis, in his transcription of the [Elgar cello concerto](/wiki/Cello_Concerto_(Elgar) "Cello Concerto (Elgar)"), wrote the slow movement with the C string tuned down to B♭, enabling the viola to play one passage an octave lower.

## Organizations and research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Viola&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Organizations and research")]

A renewal of interest in the viola by performers and composers in the twentieth century led to increased research devoted to the instrument. [Paul Hindemith](/wiki/Paul_Hindemith "Paul Hindemith") and [Vadim Borisovsky](/wiki/Vadim_Borisovsky "Vadim Borisovsky") made an early attempt at an organization, in 1927, with the Violists' World Union. But it was not until 1968, with the creation of the Viola-Forschungsgesellschaft, now the [International Viola Society](/wiki/International_Viola_Society "International Viola Society") (IVS), that a lasting organization took hold. The IVS now consists of twelve chapters around the world, the largest being the [American Viola Society](/wiki/American_Viola_Society "American Viola Society") (AVS), which publishes the *Journal of the American Viola Society*. In addition to the journal, the AVS sponsors the David Dalton Research Competition and the [Primrose International Viola Competition](/wiki/Primrose_International_Viola_Competition "Primrose International Viola Competition").

The 1960s also saw the beginning of several research publications devoted to the viola, beginning with Franz Zeyringer's *Literatur für Viola*, which has appeared in several versions, the most recent being in 1985. In 1980, Maurice Riley produced the first attempt at a comprehensive history of the viola, in his *History of the Viola*, which was followed with a second volume in 1991. The IVS published the multi-language Viola Yearbook from 1979 to 1994, during which several other national chapters of the IVS published respective newsletters. The Primrose International Viola Archive at [Brigham Young University](/wiki/Brigham_Young_University "Brigham Young University") houses the greatest amount of material related to the viola, including scores, recordings, instruments, and archival materials from some of the world's greatest violists.[[14]](#cite_note-15)

### Reading music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Viola&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Reading music")]

Music that is written for the viola primarily uses the [alto clef](/wiki/Alto_clef "Alto clef"), which is otherwise rarely used. The note that resides on the lowest line on the alto clef stave is F3, whereas in treble clef it is E4. Viola music employs the treble clef when there are substantial sections of music written in a higher register. The alto clef indicates the placement of C4 on the middle line of the staff.[[15]](#cite_note-16)

As the viola is tuned exactly one octave above the cello, music that is notated for the cello can be easily transcribed for alto clef without any changes in key. For example, there are numerous editions of [Bach's Cello Suites](/wiki/Cello_Suites_(Bach) "Cello Suites (Bach)") transcribed for viola.[[16]](#cite_note-17) The viola also has the advantage of smaller scale-length when compared to the cello. This means that the stretches needed by cellists to play certain notes are easier to achieve on the viola. However, occasional changes must be made due to differences in the ways that the two instruments are played, as well as their differences in range.

### Role in pre-twentieth century works

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Viola&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Role in pre-twentieth century works")]

In early orchestral music, the viola part was usually limited to filling in [harmonies](/wiki/Harmony "Harmony"), with very little [melodic](/wiki/Melody "Melody") material assigned to it. When the viola was given a melodic part, it was often duplicated (or was in unison with) the melody played by other strings.[[17]](#cite_note-18)

The *[Brandenburg Concertos](/wiki/Brandenburg_Concertos "Brandenburg Concertos")*, a set of concerti grossi composed by [J. S. Bach](/wiki/Johann_Sebastian_Bach "Johann Sebastian Bach"), are unusual for their time in their use of viola. The third concerto grosso, scored for three violins, three violas, three cellos, and basso continuo, requires virtuosity from the violists. Indeed, Viola I has a solo in the last movement which is commonly required in orchestral auditions.[[18]](#cite_note-19) In the sixth concerto grosso, [Brandenburg Concerto No. 6](/wiki/Brandenburg_concertos#Concerto_No._6_in_B-flat_major,_BWV_1051 "Brandenburg concertos"), scored for 2 violas "concertino", cello, 2 [violas da gamba](/wiki/Viola_da_gamba "Viola da gamba"), and continuo, the two violas play the primary melodic roles.[[19]](#cite_note-20) Bach also used this unusual ensemble in his cantatas [Gleichwie der Regen und Schnee vom Himmel fällt, BWV 18](/wiki/Gleichwie_der_Regen_und_Schnee_vom_Himmel_f%C3%A4llt,_BWV_18 "Gleichwie der Regen und Schnee vom Himmel fällt, BWV 18") and [Mein Herze schwimmt im Blut, BWV 199](/wiki/Mein_Herze_schwimmt_im_Blut,_BWV_199 "Mein Herze schwimmt im Blut, BWV 199"), in which the chorale is accompanied by an obbligato viola.

There are a few [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque") and [Classical](/wiki/Classical_Music_Era "Classical Music Era") concerti, such as those by [Georg Philipp Telemann](/wiki/Georg_Philipp_Telemann "Georg Philipp Telemann") (one [for solo viola](/wiki/Viola_Concerto_in_G_major_(Telemann) "Viola Concerto in G major (Telemann)"), being one of the earliest viola concertos known, and one [for two violas](/wiki/Concerto_for_Two_Violas_(Telemann) "Concerto for Two Violas (Telemann)")), [Alessandro Rolla](/wiki/Alessandro_Rolla "Alessandro Rolla"), [Franz Anton Hoffmeister](/wiki/Franz_Anton_Hoffmeister "Franz Anton Hoffmeister") and [Carl Stamitz](/wiki/Carl_Stamitz "Carl Stamitz").

The viola plays an important role in [chamber music](/wiki/Chamber_music "Chamber music"). Mozart used the viola in more creative ways when he wrote his six [string quintets](/wiki/String_quintet "String quintet"). The viola quintets use two violas, which frees them (especially the first viola) for solo passages and increases the variety of writing that is possible for the ensemble. Mozart also wrote for the viola in his [Sinfonia Concertante](/wiki/Sinfonia_Concertante_for_Violin,_Viola_and_Orchestra_(Mozart) "Sinfonia Concertante for Violin, Viola and Orchestra (Mozart)"), a set of two duets for violin and viola, and the [Kegelstatt Trio](/wiki/Kegelstatt_Trio "Kegelstatt Trio") for viola, clarinet, and piano. The young [Felix Mendelssohn](/wiki/Felix_Mendelssohn "Felix Mendelssohn") wrote a little-known [Viola Sonata in C minor](/wiki/Viola_Sonata_(Mendelssohn) "Viola Sonata (Mendelssohn)") (without opus number, but dating from 1824). [Robert Schumann](/wiki/Robert_Schumann "Robert Schumann") wrote his *[Märchenbilder](/wiki/M%C3%A4rchenbilder "Märchenbilder")* for viola and piano. He also wrote a set of four pieces for clarinet, viola, and piano, *[Märchenerzählungen](/wiki/M%C3%A4rchenerz%C3%A4hlungen "Märchenerzählungen")*.

[Max Bruch](/wiki/Max_Bruch "Max Bruch") wrote a romance for viola and orchestra, his Op. 85, which explores the emotive capabilities of the viola's timbre. In addition, his Eight pieces for clarinet, viola, and piano, Op. 83, features the viola in a very prominent, solo aspect throughout. His [Concerto for Clarinet, Viola, and Orchestra](/wiki/Concerto_for_Clarinet,_Viola,_and_Orchestra "Concerto for Clarinet, Viola, and Orchestra"), Op. 88 has been quite prominent in the repertoire and has been recorded by prominent violists throughout the 20th century.

From his earliest works, [Brahms](/wiki/Johannes_Brahms "Johannes Brahms") wrote music that prominently featured the viola. Among his first published pieces of chamber music, the sextets for strings Op. 18 and Op. 36 contain what amounts to solo parts for both violas. Late in life, he wrote two greatly admired [sonatas](/wiki/Clarinet_Sonatas_(Brahms) "Clarinet Sonatas (Brahms)") for clarinet and piano, his Op. 120 (1894): he later transcribed these works for the viola (the solo part in his [Horn Trio](/wiki/Horn_Trio_(Brahms) "Horn Trio (Brahms)") is also available in a transcription for viola). Brahms also wrote "[Two Songs for Voice, Viola and Piano](/wiki/Two_Songs_for_Voice,_Viola_and_Piano "Two Songs for Voice, Viola and Piano")", Op. 91, "Gestillte Sehnsucht" ("Satisfied Longing") and "Geistliches Wiegenlied" ("Spiritual Lullaby") as presents for the famous violinist [Joseph Joachim](/wiki/Joseph_Joachim "Joseph Joachim") and his wife, [Amalie](/wiki/Amalie_Joachim "Amalie Joachim"). [Dvořák](/wiki/Anton%C3%ADn_Dvo%C5%99%C3%A1k "Antonín Dvořák") played the viola and apparently said that it was his favorite instrument: his chamber music is rich in important parts for the viola. Two [Czech](/wiki/Czechs "Czechs") composers, [Bedřich Smetana](/wiki/Bed%C5%99ich_Smetana "Bedřich Smetana") and [Leoš Janáček](/wiki/Leo%C5%A1_Jan%C3%A1%C4%8Dek "Leoš Janáček"), included significant viola parts, originally written for [viola d'amore](/wiki/Viola_d%27amore "Viola d'amore"), in their quartets "[From My Life](/wiki/String_Quartet_No._1_(Smetana) "String Quartet No. 1 (Smetana)")" and "[Intimate Letters](/wiki/Intimate_Letters "Intimate Letters")" respectively: the quartets begin with an impassioned statement by the viola. This is similar to Bach, Mozart, and Beethoven all occasionally played the viola part in chamber music.

The viola occasionally has a major role in orchestral music, a prominent example being [Richard Strauss](/wiki/Richard_Strauss "Richard Strauss")' tone poem *[Don Quixote](/wiki/Don_Quixote_(Strauss) "Don Quixote (Strauss)")* for solo cello and viola and orchestra. Other examples are the "Ysobel" variation of [Edward Elgar](/wiki/Edward_Elgar "Edward Elgar")'s *[Enigma Variations](/wiki/Enigma_Variations "Enigma Variations")* and the solo in his work, *[In the South (Alassio)](/wiki/In_the_South_(Alassio) "In the South (Alassio)")*, the *[pas de deux](/wiki/Pas_de_deux "Pas de deux")* scene from act 2 of [Adolphe Adam](/wiki/Adolphe_Adam "Adolphe Adam")'s *[Giselle](/wiki/Giselle "Giselle")* and the "La Paix" movement of [Léo Delibes](/wiki/L%C3%A9o_Delibes "Léo Delibes")'s ballet *[Coppélia](/wiki/Copp%C3%A9lia "Coppélia")*, which features a lengthy viola solo.

Gabriel Fauré's [Requiem](/wiki/Requiem_(Faur%C3%A9) "Requiem (Fauré)") was originally scored (in 1888) with divided viola sections, lacking the usual violin sections, having only a solo violin for the *Sanctus*. It was later scored for orchestra with violin sections, and published in 1901. Recordings of the older scoring with violas are available.[[20]](#cite_note-21)

While the viola repertoire is quite large, the amount written by well-known pre-20th-century composers is relatively small. There are many transcriptions of works for other instruments for the viola and the large number of 20th-century compositions is very diverse. See "The Viola Project" at the San Francisco Conservatory of Music, where Professor of Viola [Jodi Levitz](/wiki/Jodi_Levitz "Jodi Levitz") has paired a composer with each of her students, resulting in a recital of brand-new works played for the very first time.[[21]](#cite_note-22)

In the earlier part of the 20th century, more composers began to write for the viola, encouraged by the emergence of specialized soloists such as [Tertis](/wiki/Lionel_Tertis "Lionel Tertis").[[22]](#cite_note-23) Englishmen [Arthur Bliss](/wiki/Arthur_Bliss "Arthur Bliss"), [Edwin York Bowen](/wiki/Edwin_York_Bowen "Edwin York Bowen"), [Benjamin Dale](/wiki/Benjamin_Dale "Benjamin Dale"), and [Ralph Vaughan Williams](/wiki/Ralph_Vaughan_Williams "Ralph Vaughan Williams") all wrote chamber and concert works for Tertis. [William Walton](/wiki/William_Walton "William Walton"), [Bohuslav Martinů](/wiki/Bohuslav_Martin%C5%AF "Bohuslav Martinů"), and [Béla Bartók](/wiki/B%C3%A9la_Bart%C3%B3k "Béla Bartók") wrote well-known viola concertos. Hindemith wrote a substantial amount of music for the viola; being himself a violist, he often performed his own works. [Claude Debussy](/wiki/Claude_Debussy "Claude Debussy")'s [Sonata for flute, viola and harp](/wiki/Sonata_for_flute,_viola_and_harp_(Debussy) "Sonata for flute, viola and harp (Debussy)") has inspired a significant number of other composers to write for this combination.

[Charles Wuorinen](/wiki/Charles_Wuorinen "Charles Wuorinen") composed his virtuosic *Viola Variations* in 2008 for Lois Martin. [Elliott Carter](/wiki/Elliott_Carter "Elliott Carter") also wrote several works for viola including his *Elegy* (1943) for viola and piano; it was subsequently transcribed for clarinet. [Ernest Bloch](/wiki/Ernest_Bloch "Ernest Bloch"), a Swiss-born American composer best known for his compositions inspired by Jewish music, wrote two famous works for viola, the *Suite 1919* and the *[Suite Hébraïque](/wiki/Suite_H%C3%A9bra%C3%AFque "Suite Hébraïque")* for solo viola and orchestra. [Rebecca Clarke](/wiki/Rebecca_Helferich_Clarke "Rebecca Helferich Clarke") was a 20th-century composer and violist who also wrote extensively for the viola.[[23]](#cite_note-24) Lionel Tertis records that Elgar (whose cello concerto Tertis transcribed for viola, with the slow movement in scordatura), [Alexander Glazunov](/wiki/Alexander_Glazunov "Alexander Glazunov") (who wrote an *Elegy*, Op. 44, for viola and piano), and [Maurice Ravel](/wiki/Maurice_Ravel "Maurice Ravel") all promised concertos for viola, yet all three died before doing any substantial work on them.

In the latter part of the 20th century a substantial repertoire was produced for the viola; many composers including [Miklós Rózsa](/wiki/Mikl%C3%B3s_R%C3%B3zsa "Miklós Rózsa"), [Revol Bunin](/wiki/Revol_Bunin "Revol Bunin"), [Alfred Schnittke](/wiki/Alfred_Schnittke "Alfred Schnittke"), [Sofia Gubaidulina](/wiki/Sofia_Gubaidulina "Sofia Gubaidulina"), [Giya Kancheli](/wiki/Giya_Kancheli "Giya Kancheli") and [Krzysztof Penderecki](/wiki/Krzysztof_Penderecki "Krzysztof Penderecki"), have written [viola concertos](/wiki/Viola_concerto "Viola concerto"). The American composer [Morton Feldman](/wiki/Morton_Feldman "Morton Feldman") wrote a series of works entitled *The Viola in My Life*, which feature [concertante](/wiki/Concertante "Concertante") viola parts. In [spectral music](/wiki/Spectral_music "Spectral music"), the viola has been sought after because of its lower overtone partials that are more easily heard than on the violin. Spectral composers like [Gérard Grisey](/wiki/G%C3%A9rard_Grisey "Gérard Grisey"), [Tristan Murail](/wiki/Tristan_Murail "Tristan Murail"), and [Horațiu Rădulescu](/wiki/Hora%C8%9Biu_R%C4%83dulescu "Horațiu Rădulescu") have written solo works for viola. Neo-Romantic, post-Modern composers have also written significant works for viola including [Robin Holloway](/wiki/Robin_Holloway "Robin Holloway") Viola Concerto Op. 56 and Sonata Op. 87, [Peter Seabourne](/wiki/Peter_Seabourne "Peter Seabourne") a large five-movement work with piano, *Pietà*, [Airat Ichmouratov](/wiki/Airat_Ichmouratov "Airat Ichmouratov") [Viola Concerto No. 1](/wiki/Viola_Concerto_No.1_(Ichmouratov) "Viola Concerto No.1 (Ichmouratov)"), Op. 7[[24]](#cite_note-25) and [Three Romances for Viola, Strings, and Harp](/wiki/Three_Romances_for_Viola,_Strings,_and_Harp "Three Romances for Viola, Strings, and Harp"), Op. 22.[[25]](#cite_note-26)

### Contemporary pop music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Viola&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Contemporary pop music")]

The viola is sometimes used in contemporary popular music, mostly in the [avant-garde](/wiki/Avant-garde "Avant-garde"). [John Cale](/wiki/John_Cale "John Cale") of [The Velvet Underground](/wiki/The_Velvet_Underground "The Velvet Underground") used the viola,[[26]](#cite_note-27) as do some modern groups such as [alternative rock](/wiki/Alternative_rock "Alternative rock") band [10,000 Maniacs](/wiki/10,000_Maniacs "10,000 Maniacs"), [Imagine Dragons](/wiki/Imagine_Dragons "Imagine Dragons"),[[27]](#cite_note-28) folk duo [John & Mary](/wiki/John_%26_Mary "John & Mary"),[[28]](#cite_note-29) [British Sea Power](/wiki/British_Sea_Power "British Sea Power"),[[29]](#cite_note-30) The Airborne Toxic Event, [Marillion](/wiki/Marillion "Marillion"), and others often with instruments in a chamber setting. [Jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") music has also seen its share of violists, from those used in string sections in the early 1900s to a handful of quartets and soloists emerging from the 1960s onward. It is quite unusual though, to use individual bowed string instruments in contemporary popular music.

## Notable violists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Viola&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Notable violists")]

Further information: [List of violists](/wiki/List_of_violists "List of violists")

There are few well-known viola virtuoso soloists, perhaps because little virtuoso viola music was written before the twentieth century. Pre-twentieth century viola players of note include Stamitz, Rolla, [Antonio Rolla](/wiki/Antonio_Rolla "Antonio Rolla"), [Chrétien Urhan](/wiki/Chr%C3%A9tien_Urhan "Chrétien Urhan"), [Casimir Ney](/wiki/Casimir_Ney "Casimir Ney"), [Louis van Waefelghem](/wiki/Louis_van_Waefelghem "Louis van Waefelghem"), and Ritter. Important viola pioneers from the twentieth century were Tertis, [William Primrose](/wiki/William_Primrose "William Primrose"), Hindemith, [Théophile Laforge](/wiki/Th%C3%A9ophile_Laforge "Théophile Laforge"), [Cecil Aronowitz](/wiki/Cecil_Aronowitz "Cecil Aronowitz"), [Maurice Vieux](/wiki/Maurice_Vieux "Maurice Vieux"), Borisovsky, [Lillian Fuchs](/wiki/Lillian_Fuchs "Lillian Fuchs"), [Dino Asciolla](/wiki/Dino_Asciolla "Dino Asciolla"), [Frederick Riddle](/wiki/Frederick_Riddle "Frederick Riddle"), [Walter Trampler](/wiki/Walter_Trampler "Walter Trampler"), [Ernst Wallfisch](/wiki/Ernst_Wallfisch "Ernst Wallfisch"), Csaba Erdélyi, the only violist to ever win the [Carl Flesch International Violin Competition](/wiki/Carl_Flesch_International_Violin_Competition "Carl Flesch International Violin Competition"),[[30]](#cite_note-31) and [Emanuel Vardi](/wiki/Emanuel_Vardi "Emanuel Vardi"), the first violist to record the [24 Caprices by Paganini](/wiki/24_Caprices_for_Solo_Violin_(Paganini) "24 Caprices for Solo Violin (Paganini)") on viola. Many noted violinists have publicly performed and recorded on the viola as well, among them [Eugène Ysaÿe](/wiki/Eug%C3%A8ne_Ysa%C3%BFe "Eugène Ysaÿe"), [Yehudi Menuhin](/wiki/Yehudi_Menuhin "Yehudi Menuhin"), [David Oistrakh](/wiki/David_Oistrakh "David Oistrakh"), [Pinchas Zukerman](/wiki/Pinchas_Zukerman "Pinchas Zukerman"), [Maxim Vengerov](/wiki/Maxim_Vengerov "Maxim Vengerov"), [Julian Rachlin](/wiki/Julian_Rachlin "Julian Rachlin"), [James Ehnes](/wiki/James_Ehnes "James Ehnes"), and [Nigel Kennedy](/wiki/Nigel_Kennedy "Nigel Kennedy").

Among the great composers, several preferred the viola to the violin when they were playing in ensembles, the most noted being [Ludwig van Beethoven](/wiki/Ludwig_van_Beethoven "Ludwig van Beethoven"), Bach[[31]](#cite_note-32) and Mozart. Other composers also chose to play the viola in ensembles, including [Joseph Haydn](/wiki/Joseph_Haydn "Joseph Haydn"), [Franz Schubert](/wiki/Franz_Schubert "Franz Schubert"), Mendelssohn, Dvořák, and [Benjamin Britten](/wiki/Benjamin_Britten "Benjamin Britten"). Among those noted both as violists and as composers are [Rebecca Clarke](/wiki/Rebecca_Clarke_(composer) "Rebecca Clarke (composer)") and Hindemith. Contemporary composers and violists [Kenji Bunch](/wiki/Kenji_Bunch "Kenji Bunch"), [Scott Slapin](/wiki/Scott_Slapin "Scott Slapin"), and [Lev Zhurbin](/wiki/Lev_Zhurbin "Lev Zhurbin") have written a number of works for viola.

## Electric violas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Viola&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Electric violas")]

Further information: [Electric violin](/wiki/Electric_violin "Electric violin")

To amplify the sound produced, regular [acoustic](/wiki/Acoustic_music "Acoustic music") violas are fitted with a [piezoelectric](/wiki/Piezoelectric "Piezoelectric") pickup. Specially-designed electric violas have little or no body;[[32]](#cite_note-Joh1-33) unlike traditional violas, they can be made of materials other than wood, and of any colour.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Electric violas are mostly violin-sized, as they use the amp and speaker to create a big sound, so they do not need a large soundbox. Indeed, some electric violas have little or no soundbox, and thus rely entirely on amplification. Fewer electric violas are available than electric violins. It can be hard for violists who prefer a physical size or familiar touch references of a viola-sized instrument, when they must use an electric viola that uses a smaller violin-sized body. [John Cale](/wiki/John_Cale "John Cale"), formerly of [The Velvet Underground](/wiki/The_Velvet_Underground "The Velvet Underground"), is one of the more notable users of such an electric viola and he has used them both for melodies in his solo work and for [drones](/wiki/Drone_(music) "Drone (music)") in his work with The Velvet Underground (e.g. "[Venus in Furs](/wiki/Venus_in_Furs_(song) "Venus in Furs (song)")"). Other notable players of the electric viola are [Geoffrey Richardson](/wiki/Geoffrey_Richardson_(musician) "Geoffrey Richardson (musician)") of [Caravan](/wiki/Caravan_(band) "Caravan (band)")[[33]](#cite_note-34) and Ramsey.[[34]](#cite_note-35)

Instruments may be built with an internal [preamplifier](/wiki/Preamplifier "Preamplifier"), or may put out an [unbuffered](/wiki/Buffer_amplifier "Buffer amplifier") [transducer](/wiki/Transducer "Transducer") [signal](/wiki/Signal_(electrical_engineering) "Signal (electrical engineering)"). While such signals may be fed directly to an amplifier or [mixing board](/wiki/Mixing_board "Mixing board"), they often benefit from an external preamp/[equalizer](/wiki/Equalization_(audio) "Equalization (audio)") on the end of a short cable, before being fed to the [sound system](/wiki/Public_address "Public address"). In rock and other loud styles, the electric viola player may use [effects units](/wiki/Effects_unit "Effects unit") such as [reverb](/wiki/Reverb "Reverb") or [overdrive](/wiki/Distortion_(music) "Distortion (music)").
